observation_summary: The system was throwing generic JsonParseException for numeric overflow errors, which lacked sufficient metadata for calling applications to distinguish between parsing errors and coercion/mismatch errors.
hypothesis: The root cause is an inadequate exception contract. The system used a generic exception type that did not convey the necessary context (input token type, target type) for coercion failures, requiring a structural change to the exception hierarchy and the reporting interface.
prediction: The code will be updated to introduce a new exception type (InputCoercionException) and the reporting methods will be updated to accept and pass this additional metadata, changing the signature/contract of the error reporting mechanism.
experiment_rationale: The fix diff shows the introduction of new methods that accept additional parameters (JsonToken inputType, Class targetType) and the replacement of generic _reportError calls with _reportInputCoercion, which confirms the change is at the interface/contract level for error handling.
reasoning_summary: The defect is classified as Interface/O-O Messages because the fix involves changing the contract of the error reporting methods to include more specific metadata (target type, input type) and introducing a new exception class. This is a communication/contract issue between the parser component and the calling application, rather than a local algorithmic error or a simple missing guard.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: While a new class was added, the primary issue was the lack of appropriate communication/metadata in the existing error reporting interface, which is better captured by Interface/O-O Messages.
The fix is not about correcting a computational formula or procedural logic, but about changing the interface and contract for how errors are reported.
